episodes.title: A Doomsday Cult Secretly Operated in the Australian Outback | And May Have Tested a Nuclear Weapon
episodes.description:
In 1993, a Japanese doomsday cult quietly purchased a remote sheep station deep in the Western Australian outback.
They told nobody why they were there.
Within weeks, scientists arrived at Banjawarn Station with specialised equipment. Sheep were brought onto the property and used to test a homemade nerve agent. Twenty nine of them died. Nobody noticed.
Then, on the night of the 28th of May 1993, something exploded in the desert with enough force to shake the earth across hundreds of kilometres. Windows rattled. The ground trembled. A truck driver reported his vehicle being lifted by a shockwave. Witnesses described a mushroom-shaped cloud on the horizon.
The Australian government investigated briefly — concluded it was probably a meteorite — and closed the case.
No meteorite was ever found.
Two years later, that same cult — Aum Shinrikyo — released sarin nerve agent across five Tokyo subway lines during the morning rush hour. Thirteen people died. Thousands were injured. It was one of the worst terrorist attacks of the twentieth century.
When a United States Senate investigation later examined what Aum Shinrikyo had been doing in Western Australia, what they found was alarming enough to include in a formal Senate report.
The cult had mined for uranium. They had the scientific expertise to attempt crude nuclear weapon construction. And the 1993 explosion — recorded on seismic monitoring equipment across the continent — has never been satisfactorily explained by any natural cause.
Australia came closer to a weapons of mass destruction attack on its own soil than most Australians will ever know.
This is the story of Banjawarn Station — and the question that has never been answered.

Disclaimer: 
The content in this video is based on the United States Senate Permanent Subcommittee on Investigations report on Aum Shinrikyo (1995), Australian Federal Police investigation findings, contemporary newspaper accounts, and publicly available historical records regarding Aum Shinrikyo's activities at Banjawarn Station, Western Australia in 1993. This video is intended for educational and historical purposes only. The nuclear explosion theory presented in this video reflects questions raised formally by the United States Senate investigation and independent researchers — it is presented as an unresolved question, not a confirmed fact. No claims are made beyond what is supported by the public record. Great Australian Mysteries does not endorse any conspiracy theory and presents all evidence with appropriate historical context. Viewer discretion is advised as this video contains discussion of terrorist attacks, weapons of mass destruction and chemical warfare.
